did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used convolutional neural networks (a deep learning method) to classify skin cancer (melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and benign tumors) based on Raman spectra analysis. The study compared the performance of these neural networks against projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis for discriminating skin tumors.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the registration of Raman spectra from 617 cases of skin neoplasms, the creation of classification models using convolutional neural networks and projection on latent structures, and the evaluation of their performance via 10-fold cross-validation and ROC AUC metrics. It reports new results comparing these methods rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of skin cancer using convolutional neural networks analysis of Raman spectra
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying skin cancer using portable Raman spectroscopy by comparing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) against projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis. The researchers analyzed in vivo Raman spectra from 617 cases of skin neoplasms, including melanomas, basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and benign tumors, collected with a 785 nm laser setup. To ensure robustness, the data was split into 80% training and 20% test sets, and model stability was assessed via 10-fold cross-validation. The results demonstrated that CNNs significantly outperformed the comparative method (p<0.01) across various classification tasks, achieving ROC AUCs of 0.96, 0.90, and 0.92 for distinguishing malignant vs. benign tumors, melanomas vs. pigmented tumors, and melanomas vs. seborrheic keratosis, respectively. The authors conclude that CNNs provide higher or comparable accuracy to trained dermatologists by more precisely accounting for low-intensity Raman bands amidst intense auto-fluorescence. These findings suggest that CNN-based analysis of Raman spectra holds promise for wide clinical implementation of optical biopsy techniques.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin tumors (malignant vs. benign, melanomas vs. pigmented tumors, melanomas vs. seborrheic keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs classification of skin cancer using convolutional neural networks on Raman spectra. Specific binary classification tasks include: a) malignant vs benign tumors, b) melanomas vs pigmented tumors, and c) melanomas vs seborrheic keratosis. The model predicts discrete class labels, evaluated via ROC AUC metrics.

datasets_used.name: In vivo Raman spectra dataset of skin neoplasms
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of in vivo Raman spectra collected using a portable Raman setup with a 785 nm laser from patients at Samara State Medical University and Samara Regional Clinical Oncology Dispensary. The dataset consists of spectra from 615 patients with 617 cases of skin neoplasms.

use.training: 80% of the spectral dataset was used for training the convolutional neural network and projection on latent structures models.
use.testing: 20% of the spectral dataset was used for testing the classification models.
use.other: 10-fold cross-validation was performed on the models to check stability.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To check the stability of the classification models.
To determine if the performance of convolutional neural networks significantly outperformed projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis.
To provide uncertainty estimation for the ROC AUC metrics obtained by the convolutional neural networks.

hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Convolutional neural networks significantly outperform projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The results demonstrate that convolutional neural networks significantly (p<0.01) outperform the projection on latent structures method across the classification tasks.

classification_or_output_head.type: Binary/Multi-class classification heads for specific tasks

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis
conclusions: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) significantly outperform projection on latent structures with discriminant analysis for classifying skin tumors using Raman spectra (p<0.01).
The performance of CNN-based classification of skin tumors based on Raman spectra analysis is higher or comparable to the accuracy provided by trained dermatologists.
The increased accuracy of CNNs is attributed to their ability to more precisely account for low-intensity Raman bands amidst an intense auto-fluorescence background.
The high performance achieved by CNN analysis of Raman spectra opens possibilities for the wide clinical implementation of portable Raman setups for skin cancer optical biopsy.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single-center dataset from Samara, Russia, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population or dataset bias.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with 413 benign tumors compared to only 12 squamous cell carcinomas, which may skew model performance for rare classes.
Specific CNN architectural details (e.g., layer counts, filter sizes, specific variants) are not provided, hindering reproducibility.
The paper does not report specific preprocessing techniques or data augmentation methods used, which are critical for evaluating robustness against noise and autofluorescence.
Performance is compared against 'trained dermatologists' without specifying the dermatologists' experience level, sample size, or the specific diagnostic criteria used for the comparison.
The claim of 'wide clinical implementation' is based on in vitro/in vivo spectral data from a controlled setup, which may not reflect real-world clinical variability and noise.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated using an 80/20 split and 10-fold cross-validation on the same dataset.
